ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bee Hoon Goreng Seafood; A0GKM (stir-fried noodle dish) used as the closest parent category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Bee Hoon Goreng Seafood weighs approximately 450 g, as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres and Indian-Muslim food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for 'Bee Hoon Goreng Seafood' as a named composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entries for fried bee hoon (rice vermicelli, stir-fried) and seafood noodle dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients (rice vermicelli, prawns, squid, egg). The input record's per_100g values (energy 144 kcal, protein 6.7 g, fat 5.6 g, carbohydrate 16.7 g) are consistent with HPB-range values for stir-fried bee hoon with protein additions and are retained with medium confidence. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C) are also consistent with HPB/USDA composite estimates and are retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bee Hoon Goreng Seafood as a composite dish. GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on the GI of rice vermicelli (white rice noodles, boiled), which is reported at approximately 58 in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care). The presence of protein (6.7 g/100g from prawns, squid, egg) and fat (5.6 g/100g) in the dish is expected to modestly attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain boiled vermicelli. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (58 × 75.15) / 100 ≈ 43.6; carbohydrate per 450g serving = 16.7 × 4.5 = 75.15 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100g; a 450g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and omit or minimise chilli paste and tomato sauce; sharing a plate or limiting portion to 200–250g is strongly advised to keep sodium intake below 900 mg per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli has a medium GI (~58) and a 450g serving provides approximately 75g carbohydrate, yielding a high glycemic load (~43); protein and fat from seafood partially attenuate the glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and request reduced tomato sauce to minimise added sugars and moderate postprandial blood glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 1.1 g/100g and trans fat is negligible at 0.02 g/100g, which are favourable; cholesterol at 55.6 mg/100g is moderate.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prioritise sodium reduction by requesting less soy sauce and tomato sauce, keep portion size to 200–250g, and note that the low saturated fat and lean seafood protein profile are otherwise consistent with a heart-healthy diet when sodium is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredients (tomato sauce, chilli paste) may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals; sodium from soy sauce can worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint sensitivity to nightshade ingredients and request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium; the lean seafood protein and low saturated fat profile are otherwise favourable for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bee Hoon Goreng Seafood is a popular Singaporean Indian-style stir-fried rice vermicelli dish featuring seafood — typically prawns and squid — along with egg, bean sprouts, cabbage, onion, and garlic, seasoned with soy sauce, tomato sauce, and chilli paste (sambal). The dish is cooked over high heat in a wok, giving the noodles a characteristic slightly charred, smoky flavour with a savoury, mildly spicy, and tangy profile. The rice vermicelli (bee hoon) provides a light, delicate noodle texture that absorbs the sauce well. Nutritionally, a 100g portion provides approximately 144 kcal, 6.7g protein, 5.6g fat (of which only 1.1g is saturated), and 16.7g carbohydrate with 1.1g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 400mg/100g, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~58) and moderate carbohydrate content require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and typically halal when prepared at Indian-Muslim hawker stalls. It is a staple of Singaporean hawker culture, commonly found at Indian-Muslim food stalls and hawker centres across the island, and is enjoyed as a lunch or dinner meal.

confidence_reason: No single HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for 'Bee Hoon Goreng Seafood' as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entries for stir-fried bee hoon and seafood noodle dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients. The input record's values are consistent with these reference ranges and are retained with medium confidence. GI is estimated by analogy from rice vermicelli GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as a named composite entry as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this specific composite dish in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.02 g/100g (trace level), consistent with small amounts from cooking oil used in stir-frying; this is not a confirmed zero but a trace value from the input record consistent with HPB-range data. All core macros (protein_g 6.7g, fat_g 5.6g, carbohydrate_g 16.7g) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a stir-fried seafood noodle dish. Glycemic load per 450g serving calculated as (58 × 75.15) / 100 ≈ 43.6, rounded to 43.4 using carbohydrate_g per serving = 16.7 × 4.5 = 75.15g. The ethnic_tag 'Drinks & Beverages' in the input record appears to be a data entry error and has been omitted from the output. Halal status is marked true as this dish is characteristically prepared at Indian-Muslim hawker stalls in Singapore; individual vendor verification is recommended.
